titlestmt.titleproper: P.J. Mode collection of persuasive cartography8548

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2025-11-04 23:59:14 UTC.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: P.J. Mode collection of persuasive cartography

origination.label: Creator
origination.corpname: Cornell University. Library. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.langmaterial: Mainly in English; some items in French, German, and Chinese.

accessrestrict.head: Restrictions on Access
accessrestrict.p: Collection is currently being processed. Material must be requested 48 hours in advance.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Collection consists of maps, graphics (such as posters, caricatures, etc.), and books and pamphlets containing maps, that are intended primarily to influence the opinion of the viewer--to send a message--rather than to communicate geographic information. The collection reflects a variety of persuasive tools: allegorical, satirical and pictorial mapping; selective inclusion or exclusion; unusual projections, graphics and text; and intentional deception. Maps in the collection address a wide range of messages: religious, political, military, commercial, moral and social.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: P.J. Mode Collection of Persuasive Cartography, #8548. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Following a career as an attorney, P.J. Mode became a map collector after viewing an exhibit of old and unusual maps at the Pompidou Center in Paris in 1980.

did.unittitle: Alcohol
Discovery of a New Continent: Or, long Winter evening's amusement

scopecontent.p: Source: Taylor, John. 1860. Drunkenness as an Indirect Cause of Crime. A Paper Read Before the National Association for the Promotion of Social Science Brandford, 1859.
